Surface low pressure moved into the western Dakotas on the afternoon of the 17th, spreading snow into portions of eastern North Dakota. Gusty southeast winds also led to low visibilities with the falling and blowing snow. Snow amounts generally ranged from four to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of mixed freezing rain and sleet quickly changed to heavy on Sunday evening. Total accumulations of one eighth of an inch of ice and from 4 to 6 inches of snow were common.  Temperatures dropped and winds increased overnight, an this event became a blizzard by Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the area of surface low pressure moved into western Minnesota, northwest winds cranked up and resulted in whiteout conditions due to falling and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy snow which had accumulated through the day on Sunday became affected by the steadily increasing winds and dropping temperatures overnight Sunday night.  By Monday morning, blizzard conditions had developed across much of eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across southern Manitoba brought a cold frontal passage along with areas of heavier snow along and north of Highway 2 and the onset of very strong pressure gradient winds across eastern North Dakota into northwest Minnesota.  The winds combined with new snow and existing loose snow pack to produce widespread blizzard conditions from late Sunday night into Monday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions through Monday morning.  New snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches, and combined with existing loose snow pack to produce whiteout conditions at times.  Most major highway systems in eastern North Dakota and the Red River Valley were closed through the forenoon and early afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a winter storm was developing over western and central North Dakota very strong winds developed across upland portions of eastern North Dakota.  The strong winds produced mainly ground blizzard conditions in the middle of the afternoon.  Eventually this entire storm system moved southeastward, and the center of the storm shifted into central South Dakota, heavier snow developed over the area and the winds decreased below blizzard force.  This continued as the Winter Storm of 14-15 April 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Loose snowpack, along with light snow and northeast winds gusting from 35 to 45 mph produced widespread ground blizzard conditions to develop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed from western North Dakota into southeastern South Dakota from Sunday the 14th into Monday the 15th.  The storm caused a few hours of  ground blizzard conditions through mid afternoon on the 14th, over portions of the state, as described in the 14 April Blizzard Episode.  From late Sunday afternoon into Monday morning, widespread heavy snow and strong winds overspread eastern North Dakota into northwest and west central Minnesota, producing form 4 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow and frequent wind gust of from 30 to 35 mph. Roadways across the area were made impassable and most area schools were either closed on Monday or had delayed start times.  Snowfall and winds tapered off slowly through the late forenoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 5 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow developed across eastern North Dakota from Sunday afternoon into early Monday morning.  In addition, blizzard force winds which had occurred through Sunday afternoon did decrease in intensity, but continued to produce frequent gusts of from 25 to 35 mph and significantly reduced visibility through the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southward into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota during the afternoon of the 6th. Meanwhile, a nice upper level disturbance moved from west to east over the front, providing a means for rapid thunderstorm development. Storms fired along Interstate 94 near Valley City and tracked southeast toward the Wahpeton/Breckenridge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered Interstate 29 near exit 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed from western North Dakota into southeastern South Dakota from Sunday the 14th into Monday the 15th.  The storm caused a few hours of  ground blizzard conditions through mid afternoon on the 14th, over portions of the state, as described in the 14 April Blizzard Episode.  From late Sunday afternoon into Monday morning, widespread heavy snow and strong winds overspread eastern North Dakota into northwest and west central Minnesota, producing form 4 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow and frequent wind gust of from 30 to 35 mph. Roadways across the area were made impassable and most area schools were either closed on Monday or had delayed start times.  Snowfall and winds tapered off slowly through the late forenoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 5 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow developed across eastern North Dakota from Sunday afternoon into early Monday morning.  In addition, blizzard force winds which had occurred through Sunday afternoon did decrease in intensity, but continued to produce frequent gusts of from 25 to 35 mph and significantly reduced visibility through the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season rapid snow melt caused flooding along the Souris (Mouse) River and Des Lacs River through north central North Dakota. Additionally, there were necessary releases from upstream dams in North Dakota and Saskatchewan, Canada, due to rapid snow melt there. Homes in the Project Road area near Burlington took on water. Many county and township roads near the river were damaged. This flood began in April and continued into June as spring rains added water to the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late season rapid snow melt caused flooding along the Souris (Mouse) River through southwest McHenry County. Additionally, there were necessary releases from upstream dams in North Dakota and Saskatchewan, Canada, due to rapid snow melt there. Some roads near the river were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought very heavy snow to a large part of eastern South Dakota and Central  Minnesota. The area of heaviest snow just grazed the far southern edge of  southeastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota from Thursday afternoon into late Thursday night, with a few areas receiving more than 6 inches of accumulation.   Moderate snowfall affected a larger area into eastern North Dakota and northwest through north-central Minnesota into early Friday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across southwestern Sargent County, while from 3-5 inches fell across northeastern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a winter storm was developing over western and central North Dakota very strong winds developed across upland portions of eastern North Dakota.  The strong winds produced mainly ground blizzard conditions in the middle of the afternoon.  Eventually this entire storm system moved southeastward, and the center of the storm shifted into central South Dakota, heavier snow developed over the area and the winds decreased below blizzard force.  This continued as the Winter Storm of 14-15 April 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Loose snowpack, along with light snow and northeast winds gusting from 35 to 45 mph produced widespread ground blizzard conditions to develop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along the North Dakota/South Dakota border south of Jamestown began to bow as they reached Ransom and Sargent Counties in southeast North Dakota. From this area they tracked eastward toward Fargo and Wahpeton, where they weakened a little. As the storms moved into west central Minnesota, they began to strengthen again as they moved across southern Otter Tail and southern Wadena counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inch diameter tree branches were blown down and corn was flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along the North Dakota/South Dakota border south of Jamestown began to bow as they reached Ransom and Sargent Counties in southeast North Dakota. From this area they tracked eastward toward Fargo and Wahpeton, where they weakened a little. As the storms moved into west central Minnesota, they began to strengthen again as they moved across southern Otter Tail and southern Wadena counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A five inch diameter branch was blown down across a road in south Fargo. Other small branches were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By late in the afternoon of the 31st, a strong thunderstorm formed near Blackduck, Minnesota, and produced very strong winds. Meanwhile, a weak area of surface low pressure over northeast North Dakota combined with a cold front to produce a weak tornado near Crystal. As the cold front pushed into far southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, additional strong thunderstorms fired up, producing more hail and strong wind gusts. As evening approached, an upper level disturbance dropped southeast out of Manitoba, Canada, and helped sustain a few more strong thunderstorms from Pembina toward the Lake of the Woods.

Meanwhile, a weak area of surface low pressure over northeast North Dakota combined with a cold front to produce a weak tornado near Crystal. As the cold front pushed into far southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, additional strong thunderstorms fired up, producing more hail and strong wind gusts. As evening approached, an upper level disturbance dropped southeast out of Manitoba, Canada, and helped sustain a few more strong thunderstorms from Pembina toward the Lake of the Woods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed first. Then dust was seen being drawn into the circulation from the ground. The tornado tracked intermittently for about a mile with peak winds estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a winter storm was developing over western and central North Dakota very strong winds developed across upland portions of eastern North Dakota.  The strong winds produced mainly ground blizzard conditions in the middle of the afternoon.  Eventually this entire storm system moved southeastward, and the center of the storm shifted into central South Dakota, heavier snow developed over the area and the winds decreased below blizzard force.  This continued as the Winter Storm of 14-15 April 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Loose snowpack, along with light snow and northeast winds gusting from 35 to 45 mph produced widespread ground blizzard conditions to develop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed from western North Dakota into southeastern South Dakota from Sunday the 14th into Monday the 15th.  The storm caused a few hours of  ground blizzard conditions through mid afternoon on the 14th, over portions of the state, as described in the 14 April Blizzard Episode.  From late Sunday afternoon into Monday morning, widespread heavy snow and strong winds overspread eastern North Dakota into northwest and west central Minnesota, producing form 4 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow and frequent wind gust of from 30 to 35 mph. Roadways across the area were made impassable and most area schools were either closed on Monday or had delayed start times.  Snowfall and winds tapered off slowly through the late forenoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 5 to 14 inches of heavy wet snow developed across eastern North Dakota from Sunday afternoon into early Monday morning.  In addition, blizzard force winds which had occurred through Sunday afternoon did decrease in intensity, but continued to produce frequent gusts of from 25 to 35 mph and significantly reduced visibility through the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture laden Colorado Low tracked from central Nebraska on the morning of the 10th to central Lake Superior by the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the system there was some light freezing rain and fog early as temperatures were quite mild. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 21 inches, with the most snow falling across extreme southeast North Dakota. The falling snow combined with north to northwest winds at 35 to 45 mph to produce whiteout conditions. Interstate 29 was closed from Grand Forks to the South Dakota border, Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown to Fargo, and North Dakota 13 was closed around Wahpeton. Many schools and other activities closed down on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a round of about one to five inches of light snow fell across eastern North Dakota on December 2nd and 3rd, a second round of snow fell on the 3rd through the 4th. This second batch of snow set up along an inverted trough, bringing about 4 to 10 inches of snow to the area. Slightly higher amounts, about 6 to 10 inches, fell along and north of the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. In addition to the snow, some light freezing rain occurred over portions of southeast North Dakota. Northerly winds also became quite gusty, producing some blowing and drifting snow with visibilities below a quarter mile at times. Many schools were closed and other activities were cancelled.

Frigid air moved into eastern North Dakota on the 31st along a thin band of clearing. Temperatures fell into the teens below zero with steady northwest winds, which kept wind chill readings around 40 below to 45 below zero. Temperatures did not show much recovery during the day hours and winds remained steady. Due to this fact, the wind chill warning continued through February 1st.

The 12th was a hot and humid day across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Afternoon temperatures topped out in the 80s with dew points around 70F. A weak convergent boundary, which by late afternoon extended from near Roseau south-southwest to a surface low near Aberdeen, South Dakota, provided the focus for the initial thunderstorm development. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were received, but the most extensive damage occurred in the Thief River Falls, Minnesota, area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought a wide variety of severe winter weather to the Northern Plains region the 11th and 12th. ||The first wave of winter weather arrived during the morning hours of the 11th in the form of light freezing rain. This resulted in a light coating of ice on roads making travel hazardous across west and central North Dakota. The precipitation eventually turned over to all snow later in the morning. A band of heavy snow developed early in the afternoon across south central North Dakota, extending north and east into the northern Red River Valley by early in the evening of the 11th. The snow ended from the west during the morning hours of the 12th. Up to six inches of snow fell over parts of south central North Dakota.||Another area of heavy snow over eastern Montana on the 11th eventually developed east into northwest and north central North Dakota, where up to six inches of snow fell.||In addition to the snow, strong north winds gusting to over 35 miles per hour at times created widespread blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities during the evening of the 11th through the morning hours of the 12th. The worst conditions occurred over northwest and far north central North Dakota where blizzard conditions were observed for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds in excess of 35 miles per hour, combined with falling snow and existing loose snow cover on the ground, resulted in several hours of near zero visibilities.

No travel was advised by local and state officials across much of the warned area. Conditions quickly improved throughout the evening as winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow combined with northwest winds gusting to 45 miles per hour resulted in a blizzard during the late afternoon and early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a round of about one to five inches of light snow fell across eastern North Dakota on December 2nd and 3rd, a second round of snow fell on the 3rd through the 4th. This second batch of snow set up along an inverted trough, bringing about 4 to 10 inches of snow to the area. Slightly higher amounts, about 6 to 10 inches, fell along and north of the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. In addition to the snow, some light freezing rain occurred over portions of southeast North Dakota. Northerly winds also became quite gusty, producing some blowing and drifting snow with visibilities below a quarter mile at times. Many schools were closed and other activities were cancelled.

An Arctic Airmass building across the Northern Plains brought sub-zero temperatures and winds up to twenty miles per hour from the 20th into the morning of the 21st.  Wind chill temperatures ranged from nearly fifty below zero across north central North Dakota, to around 40 below farther to the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures of forty below to near fifty below zero were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Northern Plains brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations to the southern two thirds of western and central North Dakota. The highest snow accumulations ranged from ten inches at Rhame in southwest North Dakota, to nine inches east of Carrington in the northern James River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A reported snowfall of ten inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked from the central plains on the morning of the 11th to the Minnesota arrowhead by the morning of the 12th. This system brought 3 to 8 inches of snow to the region. As north to northwest wind speeds gusted from 35 mph to 50 mph later on the 11th through the morning of the 12th, whiteout conditions occurred.

A strong winter storm brought a wide variety of severe winter weather to the Northern Plains region the 11th and 12th. ||The first wave of winter weather arrived during the morning hours of the 11th in the form of light freezing rain. This resulted in a light coating of ice on roads making travel hazardous across west and central North Dakota. The precipitation eventually turned over to all snow later in the morning. A band of heavy snow developed early in the afternoon across south central North Dakota, extending north and east into the northern Red River Valley by early in the evening of the 11th. The snow ended from the west during the morning hours of the 12th. Up to six inches of snow fell over parts of south central North Dakota.||Another area of heavy snow over eastern Montana on the 11th eventually developed east into northwest and north central North Dakota, where up to six inches of snow fell.||In addition to the snow, strong north winds gusting to over 35 miles per hour at times created widespread blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities during the evening of the 11th through the morning hours of the 12th. The worst conditions occurred over northwest and far north central North Dakota where blizzard conditions were observed for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds in excess of 35 miles per hour, combined with falling snow and existing loose snow cover on the ground, resulted in several hours of near zero visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure building into the Northern Plains brought a prolonged period of dangerous wind chill temperatures to the area. Wind chills as low as 55 below zero were recorded.||This wind chill event continued into February 1 for parts of the area. Information is included in February 2013 Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind Chill temperatures around fifty below zero were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure building into the Northern Plains brought a prolonged period of dangerous wind chill temperatures to the area. A moisture laden Colorado Low tracked from central Nebraska on the morning of the 10th to central Lake Superior by the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the system there was some light freezing rain and fog early as temperatures were quite mild. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches. The falling snow combined with north to northwest winds at 30 to 40 mph to produce occasional visibilities below a half mile. Interstate 29 was closed from Grand Forks to the South Dakota border. Many other roads were also closed which resulted in cancelled schools and other activities on the 11th.

Winds gusting to 50 miles per hour combined with falling snow and new snow accumulations of two inches to create a blizzard from the late morning across the north and into the evening across the James River Valley. Numerous no-travel advisories were issued by state and county officials as conditions deteriorated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow combined with northwest winds gusting to 50 miles per hour resulted in a blizzard during the afternoon. No travel was advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a round of about one to five inches of light snow fell across eastern North Dakota on December 2nd and 3rd, a second round of snow fell on the 3rd through the 4th. This second batch of snow set up along an inverted trough, bringing about 4 to 10 inches of snow to the area. Slightly higher amounts, about 6 to 10 inches, fell along and north of the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. In addition to the snow, some light freezing rain occurred over portions of southeast North Dakota. Northerly winds also became quite gusty, producing some blowing and drifting snow with visibilities below a quarter mile at times. Many schools were closed and other activities were cancelled.

A strong cold front moving out of Canada and into the Northern Plains resulted in sustained winds to around 40 miles per hour and peak gusts to near 60 miles per hour over far north central North Dakota. The wind, combined with two inches of loose snow cover, resulted in a ground blizzard around the Turtle Mountain region on the 19th. To the west of this area, where there was very little loose snow cover, high winds were reported but no blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds in excess of 35 miles per hour combined with loose snow cover to produce several hours of near zero visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked from the central plains on the morning of the 11th to the Minnesota arrowhead by the morning of the 12th. This system brought 2 to 4 inches of snow and some light freezing rain to the region. As north to northwest wind speeds gusted from 30 mph to 40 mph later on the 11th through the morning of the 12th, lower visibilities due to blowing snow also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Falling snow combined with northwest winds gusting to 45 miles per hour to create a blizzard during the late afternoon and evening of the 9th across southwest and into far south central North Dakota. No travel was advised by local and state officials across much of the warned area. Conditions quickly improved throughout the evening as winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow combined with northwest winds gusting to 45 miles per hour resulted in a blizzard during the late afternoon and evening. No travel was advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought a wide variety of severe winter weather to the Northern Plains region the 11th and 12th. ||The first wave of winter weather arrived during the morning hours of the 11th in the form of light freezing rain. This resulted in a light coating of ice on roads making travel hazardous across west and central North Dakota. The precipitation eventually turned over to all snow later in the morning. A band of heavy snow developed early in the afternoon across south central North Dakota, extending north and east into the northern Red River Valley by early in the evening of the 11th. The snow ended from the west during the morning hours of the 12th. Up to six inches of snow fell over parts of south central North Dakota.||Another area of heavy snow over eastern Montana on the 11th eventually developed east into northwest and north central North Dakota, where up to six inches of snow fell.||In addition to the snow, strong north winds gusting to over 35 miles per hour at times created widespread blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities during the evening of the 11th through the morning hours of the 12th. The worst conditions occurred over northwest and far north central North Dakota where blizzard conditions were observed for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported at Gackle.

Storms fired along Interstate 94 near Valley City and tracked southeast toward the Wahpeton/Breckenridge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar sized hail fell near Litchville.

A band of heavy snow developed early in the afternoon across south central North Dakota, extending north and east into the northern Red River Valley by early in the evening of the 11th. The snow ended from the west during the morning hours of the 12th. Up to six inches of snow fell over parts of south central North Dakota.||Another area of heavy snow over eastern Montana on the 11th eventually developed east into northwest and north central North Dakota, where up to six inches of snow fell.||In addition to the snow, strong north winds gusting to over 35 miles per hour at times created widespread blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities during the evening of the 11th through the morning hours of the 12th. The worst conditions occurred over northwest and far north central North Dakota where blizzard conditions were observed for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds in excess of 35 miles per hour, combined with falling snow and existing loose snow cover on the ground, resulted in several hours of near zero visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subzero temperatures combined with winds up to 15 miles per hour to produce wind chill temperatures as low as fifty below zero during the evening of the 6th and the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures as low as 47 below zero were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Northern Plains brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations to the southern two thirds of western and central North Dakota. The highest snow accumulations ranged from ten inches at Rhame in southwest North Dakota, to nine inches east of Carrington in the northern James River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated snowfall of six inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting northeast across the Central Plains brought a band of heavy snow to the far southern James River Basin of North Dakota during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. Snow eventually ended early on the morning of the 29th.  A storm total snowfall of eight inches was reported at both Ellendale and Oakes, Dickey County, and seven inches near Verona, LaMoure County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total snowfall amount of eight inches was reported at Oakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture laden Colorado Low tracked from central Nebraska on the morning of the 10th to central Lake Superior by the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the system there was some light freezing rain and fog early as temperatures were quite mild. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 21 inches, with the most snow falling across extreme southeast North Dakota. The falling snow combined with north to northwest winds at 35 to 45 mph to produce whiteout conditions. Interstate 29 was closed from Grand Forks to the South Dakota border, Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown to Fargo, and North Dakota 13 was closed around Wahpeton. Many schools and other activities closed down on the 11th.

Frigid air moved into eastern North Dakota on the 31st along a thin band of clearing. Temperatures fell into the teens below zero with steady northwest winds, which kept wind chill readings around 40 below to 45 below zero. Temperatures did not show much recovery during the day hours and winds remained steady. Due to this fact, the wind chill warning continued through February 1st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch diameter tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 12th was a hot and humid day across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Afternoon temperatures topped out in the 80s with dew points around 70F. A weak convergent boundary, which by late afternoon extended from near Roseau south-southwest to a surface low near Aberdeen, South Dakota, provided the focus for the initial thunderstorm development. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were received, but the most extensive damage occurred in the Thief River Falls, Minnesota, area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell along U. S. Highway 2 on the north side of Grand Forks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the area of surface low pressure moved into western Minnesota, northwest winds cranked up and resulted in whiteout conditions due to falling and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy snow which had accumulated through the day on Sunday became affected by the steadily increasing winds and dropping temperatures overnight Sunday night.  By Monday morning, blizzard conditions had developed across much of eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across southern Manitoba brought a cold frontal passage along with areas of heavier snow along and north of Highway 2 and the onset of very strong pressure gradient winds across eastern North Dakota into northwest Minnesota.  The winds combined with new snow and existing loose snow pack to produce widespread blizzard conditions from late Sunday night into Monday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions through Monday morning.  New snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches, and combined with existing loose snow pack to produce whiteout conditions at times.  Most major highway systems in eastern North Dakota and the Red River Valley were closed through the forenoon and early afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moved into the western Dakotas on the afternoon of the 17th, spreading snow into portions of eastern North Dakota. Gusty southeast winds also led to low visibilities with the falling and blowing snow. Snow amounts generally ranged from four to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of mixed freezing rain and sleet was followed quickly by a prolonged period of heavy, wet snow on Sunday evening.  Snow accumulations of from 6 to 8 inches were common.  The following morning, as temperatures had dropped and winds increased this event became a blizzard on Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season rapid snow melt caused flooding along the Souris (Mouse) River and Des Lacs River through north central North Dakota. Additionally, there were necessary releases from upstream dams in North Dakota and Saskatchewan, Canada, due to rapid snow melt there. Homes in the Project Road area near Burlington took on water. Many county and township roads near the river were damaged. This flood continued through May and into June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late season rapid snow melt caused flooding along the Souris (Mouse) River and Des Lacs River through Ward County. Many county and township roads near the river were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across southern Manitoba brought a cold frontal passage along with areas of heavier snow along and north of Highway 2 and the onset of very strong pressure gradient winds across eastern North Dakota into northwest Minnesota.  The winds combined with new snow and existing loose snow pack to produce widespread blizzard conditions from late Sunday night into Monday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions through Monday morning.  New snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches, and combined with existing loose snow pack to produce whiteout conditions at times.  Most major highway systems in eastern North Dakota and the Red River Valley were closed through the forenoon and early afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the area of surface low pressure moved into western Minnesota, northwest winds cranked up and resulted in whiteout conditions due to falling and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy snow which had accumulated through the day on Sunday became affected by the steadily increasing winds and dropping temperatures overnight Sunday night.  By Monday morning, blizzard conditions had developed across much of eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley.

A moisture laden Colorado Low tracked from central Nebraska on the morning of the 10th to central Lake Superior by the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the system there was some light freezing rain and fog early as temperatures were quite mild. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 21 inches, with the most snow falling across extreme southeast North Dakota. Interstate 29 was closed from Grand Forks to the South Dakota border, Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown to Fargo, and North Dakota 13 was closed around Wahpeton. Many schools and other activities closed down on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across southwest North Dakota late in the morning of the 21st, and continued into far south central North Dakota through the early afternoon before becoming more focused over South Dakota. Low level warm air advection combined with a subtle mid level short wave impulse rotating through the region to trigger the severe convection. Large hail and severe thunderstorm wind gusts accompanied the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain accompanied the strong winds.

Severe Thunderstorm Watch numbers 345 and 350 were in effect during this event.||A capped atmosphere and weak bulk shear prevented convection over North Dakota while storms were developing over far southeast Montana and eastern Wyoming.||Later in the afternoon bulk shear increased substantially into North Dakota in the vicinity of a southwest to northeast orientated surface trough. As the cap weakened, convection became more organized over south central North Dakota early in the evening. In addition, a bowing line segment originating over northwest South Dakota, propagated northeast through the evening hours along the surface trough axis into Adams County, North Dakota, eventually moved into the Bismarck area and then toward the western Devils Lake Basin region.||Numerous reports of large hail, severe thunderstorm wind gusts, and heavy rain accompanied the severe thunderstorms. Flash flood warnings were posted for a large area of south central North Dakota, where several periods of thunderstorms with heavy rain moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong winds took the roof off of a partially built building.  Debris from the roof damaged a parked car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level low pressure over central Saskatchewan resulted in steep lapse rates near the Canadian border and led to isolated strong thunderstorms over northwest North Dakota. A funnel cloud was reported near Crosby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported one mile west of Crosby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave trough moving into the Northern Plains combined with afternoon instability to trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms along a weak surface boundary over south central North Dakota.  An isolated supercell produced large hail and severe thunderstorm winds as it tracked southeast from Burleigh County through Dickey County before moving into South Dakota by the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds to fifty miles per hour accompanied the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon of the 3rd over southwestern North Dakota resulted in increasing instability in the late afternoon and early evening, and led to discrete non-supercellular thunderstorms developing along a weak surface boundary. Vertically stacked low pressure over eastern Montana generating strong low level wind shear across southwestern North Dakota resulting in several brief weak tornadoes with these storms. All of the tornadoes were rated as EF0.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado touchdown near Bowman, reported by North Dakota State Water Commission.  There were no reports of damage so the tornado was rated EF0.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down briefly in open country north of the city of Hettinger. No damage was reported so this tornado was rated EF0.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado knocked down six inch in diameter trees on a rural ranch.  This tornado was rated as EF0 with estimated wind speeds around 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed along a southeastward advancing cold front over northwest North Dakota during the evening of the 9th. These storms produced severe thunderstorm wind and non-severe hail.

Scattered thunderstorms formed along a southeastward advancing cold front over northwest North Dakota during the evening of the 9th. These storms produced severe thunderstorm wind and non-severe hail.

Severe Thunderstorm Watch numbers 345 and 350 were in effect during this event.||A capped atmosphere and weak bulk shear prevented convection over North Dakota while storms were developing over far southeast Montana and eastern Wyoming.||Later in the afternoon bulk shear increased substantially into North Dakota in the vicinity of a southwest to northeast orientated surface trough. As the cap weakened, convection became more organized over south central North Dakota early in the evening. In addition, a bowing line segment originating over northwest South Dakota, propagated northeast through the evening hours along the surface trough axis into Adams County, North Dakota, eventually moved into the Bismarck area and then toward the western Devils Lake Basin region.||Numerous reports of large hail, severe thunderstorm wind gusts, and heavy rain accompanied the severe thunderstorms. Flash flood warnings were posted for a large area of south central North Dakota, where several periods of thunderstorms with heavy rain moved through.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain, an inch in ten minutes reported from south Bismarck, fell across Bismarck late in the evening of June 22nd. Numerous reports of flash flooding were received from across the city, including the intersection of Washington and Divide, 26th and Rosser, and at points along Bismarck Expressway including at Washington. These intersections had the worst flooding with up to a foot of water flowing across the road, over the curb, and in some cases into homes and businesses. Underpasses were flooded with several feet of water, and one road was closed due to fast flowing deep water. Vehicles were stalled at various locations in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across southwest North Dakota late in the morning of the 21st, and continued into far south central North Dakota through the early afternoon before becoming more focused over South Dakota. Low level warm air advection combined with a subtle mid level short wave impulse rotating through the region to trigger the severe convection. Large hail and severe thunderstorm wind gusts accompanied the storms.

Clearing skies in the afternoon of the 3rd over southwestern North Dakota resulted in increasing instability in the late afternoon and early evening, and led to discrete non-supercellular thunderstorms developing along a weak surface boundary. Vertically stacked low pressure over eastern Montana generating strong low level wind shear across southwestern North Dakota resulting in several brief weak tornadoes with these storms. All of the tornadoes were rated as EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An instability axis stretching from northeast Montana through western North Dakota was the focus for evening thunderstorms as a mid level short wave pushed across the western Dakotas.||Storms initially remained sub-severe early in the evening, producing non-severe hail across Williams and Billings Counties.  A small-scale supercell thunderstorm increased in strength as it approached the city of Dickinson in Stark County, and produced several microbursts from the west side of Dickinson, southeast across south central Stark County and into eastern Hettinger County. Winds up to 90 miles per hour were estimated from a storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dickinson Airport manager indicated there was no damage at or around the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An instability axis stretching from northeast Montana through western North Dakota was the focus for evening thunderstorms as a mid level short wave pushed across the western Dakotas.||Storms initially remained sub-severe early in the evening, producing non-severe hail across Williams and Billings Counties.  A small-scale supercell thunderstorm increased in strength as it approached the city of Dickinson in Stark County, and produced several microbursts from the west side of Dickinson, southeast across south central Stark County and into eastern Hettinger County. Winds up to 90 miles per hour were estimated from a storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey was done on the west side of Dickinson where wind damage occurred. The very high winds blew the roof off a storage unit and blew doors in. The debris from this building was blown to the southeast, and resulted in additional damage to several other buildings and vehicles. The damage surveyed was consistent with straight line winds and not a tornado.  The damage surveyed indicated peak wind speeds of approximately 90 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave moving from Saskatchewan into North Dakota triggered afternoon and evening thunderstorms along an instability axis over western North Dakota. Deep layer wind shear around 35 knots combined with daytime destabilization to create an environment favorable for a few sustained thunderstorm updrafts. Large hail and severe thunderstorm winds accompanied the storms.

Severe Thunderstorm Watch number 506 was in effect during this event.||A prefrontal surface trough stretched from north to south across western North Dakota and was the focus for convective initialization in the mid to late afternoon hours as subtle mid level impulses in zonal flow aloft moved into the Northern Plains.||Storms initially developed near the Canadian border over extreme southeast Saskatchewan, where the prefrontal trough intersected a surface cold front. This complex of storms remained just north of the International Border before becoming supercellular and turning southeast into far north central North Dakota. Another complex of storms developed across eastern Stark County.  Eventually the entire trough axis filled in with convection, with both right and left moving supercells spreading eastward throughout the evening. The numerous severe thunderstorms brought widespread large hail and severe thunderstorm wind gusts to many locations.

Severe Thunderstorm Watch number 500 was in effect during this event. ||Strong to severe thunderstorms, which developed during the late afternoon and evening in eastern Montana, organized into a north to south band of storms as they approached far western North Dakota. A short wave trough riding over an upper level ridge across northern Montana proved to be the trigger mechanism for convection, which was sustained across west and central North Dakota through the early morning hours of the 29th. The severe thunderstorms produced widespread severe wind gusts and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain accompanied the strong winds.

A mid level short wave tracking along the North and South Dakota border was the focus for thunderstorm development late in the afternoon hours. A stronger storm produced sub-severe hail in eastern Sioux County in far south central North Dakota.

Severe thunderstorms developing over eastern Montana mostly dissipated as they moved into western North Dakota as they encountered a stronger mid level capping inversion. One storm held together and produced a severe thunderstorm wind gust in Bowman County. A brief funnel cloud was also reported in far northwestern Ward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm wind gust was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm moving through the James River Valley produced sub-severe hail at Jamestown.

An instability axis stretching from northeast Montana through western North Dakota was the focus for evening thunderstorms as a mid level short wave pushed across the western Dakotas.||Storms initially remained sub-severe early in the evening, producing non-severe hail across Williams and Billings Counties.  A small-scale supercell thunderstorm increased in strength as it approached the city of Dickinson in Stark County, and produced several microbursts from the west side of Dickinson, southeast across south central Stark County and into eastern Hettinger County. Winds up to 90 miles per hour were estimated from a storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service.

Tornado Watch number 428 was in effect during this event. ||An eastward moving surface cold front and upper level shortwave trough combined with strong instability and wind shear to trigger a severe weather outbreak across much of west and central North Dakota.  Storms initiated during the mid afternoon over northwest North Dakota, and continued to develop east and south through the mid evening hours.  Originally, the Tornado Watch was mainly north of Interstate 94, but was later expanded southward as severe storms moved southeast, and as thunderstorms over south central North Dakota became severe.  Very large hail, severe thunderstorm wind gusts, and very heavy rain accompanied the storms. In addition, one confirmed tornado rated EF0 briefly touched down in rural McHenry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail damaged vehicles and homes in the city of Halliday.

A weak cold front draped from south central into southeast North Dakota was the focus for severe convection from the mid afternoon through the early evening. Isolated severe thunderstorms initially developed over Oliver County south of Lake Sakakawea, then tracked east southeast along the cold front into the southern James River Valley. One confirmed tornado, rated as an EF0, occurred over central Morton County north of Sweet Briar Lake.  Besides this weak tornado, the severe thunderstorms also produced large hail and severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

Lead shortwave impulses ejecting across the Northern Plains from a northern Rocky Mountain trough triggered strong to severe convection from eastern Montana into parts of North Dakota during the late afternoon and early evening. The strongest storms occurred along a west to east orientated surface boundary over northwest and north central North Dakota where they produced severe thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain accompanied the strong winds.

A weak frontal boundary north of Interstate 94 in south central North Dakota moved slowly south late in the evening of the 30th and into the early morning of the 31st. Enough elevated instability and bulk wind shear was present to trigger a severe thunderstorm over the Bismarck and Mandan metropolitan area where it produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of large hail.

Lead shortwave impulses ejecting across the Northern Plains from a northern Rocky Mountain trough triggered strong to severe convection from eastern Montana into parts of North Dakota during the late afternoon and early evening. The strongest storms occurred along a west to east orientated surface boundary over northwest and north central North Dakota where they produced severe thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A camper was blown over four miles west of Powers Lake on Highway 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornado Watch number 428 was in effect during this event. ||An eastward moving surface cold front and upper level shortwave trough combined with strong instability and wind shear to trigger a severe weather outbreak across much of west and central North Dakota.  Storms initiated during the mid afternoon over northwest North Dakota, and continued to develop east and south through the mid evening hours.  Originally, the Tornado Watch was mainly north of Interstate 94, but was later expanded southward as severe storms moved southeast, and as thunderstorms over south central North Dakota became severe.  Very large hail, severe thunderstorm wind gusts, and very heavy rain accompanied the storms. In addition, one confirmed tornado rated EF0 briefly touched down in rural McHenry County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorm Watch numbers 402 and 403 were in effect during this event.||A mid level shortwave trough moving east over north central Montana was the trigger for afternoon severe convection over central and eastern Montana. Widespread thunderstorms continued to track east into western North Dakota early in the evening and maintained their intensity due to a moderately unstable air mass over North Dakota. Large hail and severe thunderstorm wind gusts accompanied this initial surge of severe storms. By the mid evening the storms congealed into an eastward moving mesoscale convective system, where it produced severe thunderstorm winds across south central North Dakota and into the James River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of up to eight inch in diameter trees snapped around Killdeer.  A hanger at the Killdeer airport was destroyed, with several planes damaged or destroyed.

Severe Thunderstorm Watch number 506 was in effect during this event.||A prefrontal surface trough stretched from north to south across western North Dakota and was the focus for convective initialization in the mid to late afternoon hours as subtle mid level impulses in zonal flow aloft moved into the Northern Plains.||Storms initially developed near the Canadian border over extreme southeast Saskatchewan, where the prefrontal trough intersected a surface cold front. This complex of storms remained just north of the International Border before becoming supercellular and turning southeast into far north central North Dakota. Another complex of storms developed across eastern Stark County.  Eventually the entire trough axis filled in with convection, with both right and left moving supercells spreading eastward throughout the evening. The numerous severe thunderstorms brought widespread large hail and severe thunderstorm wind gusts to many locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of surface low pressure drifted to near Bismarck, North Dakota, by the early morning hours of the 4th and stalled there until the early morning hours of the 5th. This resulted in a prolonged period of snow over most of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Several stations in northeast North Dakota reported snow amounts of over a foot, with the Sarles (ND) station reporting 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 5 to 8 inches affected most of east central North Dakota. Areas of light freezing rain preceded the snow on Sunday evening leading to very icy roads and walkways.  Blowing and drifting snow persisted into Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted west and central North Dakota March 3rd into the evening of March 4th. Heavy snow fell northwest, north central, and into the northern James River Valley, while blizzard conditions developed March 4th across much of the rest of the west and south central parts of the state.||Initially the heavy snow was confined to the northwest, north central, and into Foster County, where six to twelve inches fell. The heaviest snow, one foot, was reported around Minot.||As the storm moved east, very strong northwest winds up to 55 miles per hour developed across the northwest during the afternoon of the 4th. The strong winds then continued to develop south across the remainder of western North Dakota during the afternoon and eventually into south central North Dakota during the evening hours. Wrap around snow accompanied the strong winds, with seven inches of snow reported across the far southwest, tapering off as you moved east along the South Dakota border.||The heavy snow and blizzard conditions resulted in the North Dakota Department of Transportation issuing no travel advisories for all of northern and western North Dakota at the peak of the storm.  Parts of Highway 85 and Highway 50 were closed in Williams and Divide Counties due to heavy snow drifts blocking the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall up to ten inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong frontogenesis developed along a southward advancing cold front and extended from southern Montana into western North Dakota. Areas west and south of the Missouri River received the most snow, ranging from three inches just west of the river, to a foot of snow at Beach on the Montana border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of twelve inches was reported.

Areas west and south of the Missouri River received the most snow, ranging from three inches just west of the river, to a foot of snow at Beach on the Montana border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to ten inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of surface low pressure drifted to near Bismarck, North Dakota, by the early morning hours of the 4th and stalled there until the early morning hours of the 5th. This resulted in a prolonged period of snow over most of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Several stations in northeast North Dakota reported snow amounts of over a foot, with the Sarles (ND) station reporting 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 8 to 18 inches fell across northeast northeastern North Dakota from late on Sunday evening into Monday night.  Additional blowing an drifting snow continued into early Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted west and central North Dakota March 3rd into the evening of March 4th. Heavy snow fell northwest, north central, and into the northern James River Valley, while blizzard conditions developed March 4th across much of the rest of the west and south central parts of the state.||Initially the heavy snow was confined to the northwest, north central, and into Foster County, where six to twelve inches fell. The heaviest snow, one foot, was reported around Minot.||As the storm moved east, very strong northwest winds up to 55 miles per hour developed across the northwest during the afternoon of the 4th. The strong winds then continued to develop south across the remainder of western North Dakota during the afternoon and eventually into south central North Dakota during the evening hours. Wrap around snow accompanied the strong winds, with seven inches of snow reported across the far southwest, tapering off as you moved east along the South Dakota border.||The heavy snow and blizzard conditions resulted in the North Dakota Department of Transportation issuing no travel advisories for all of northern and western North Dakota at the peak of the storm.  Parts of Highway 85 and Highway 50 were closed in Williams and Divide Counties due to heavy snow drifts blocking the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall up to ten inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure pushed across the Rockies April 13th, slowed down over the Dakotas April 14th, then moved east on April 15th. The peak of the storm occurred on Sunday when much of west and central North Dakota received heavy amounts of snow, with portions of the south central and southeast having a blizzard.||A combination of favorable moisture feed and strong surface and mid level dynamics brought historic snowfall to parts of west and central North Dakota. A moist conveyer belt extended from the eastern portions of Iowa and Illinois back northwest into North Dakota, situated along and north of an inverted surface trough stretched from southeast to northwest across South Dakota. Combined with strong diffluent flow aloft associated with the upper level low, snowfall rates approached two inches per hour across south central North Dakota on Sunday.||In addition to the heavy snow, strong surface winds developed across south central North Dakota west of the Missouri River and into the James River Valley. Wind gusts to 40 miles per hour combined with the heavy snow resulting in a blizzard.||Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 15 to 22 inches from the James River Valley into the Missouri River Valley. Lesser amounts were reported across the far west and north, with up to eight inches at Watford City, and around an inch near the Canadian border. The highest snowfall amounts were reported at Center and Flasher with 22 inches.  A record snowfall for any calendar day of the year was set at Bismarck, with 15.5 inches falling on April 14th. The storm total in Bismarck was 17.7 inches. This also resulted in setting a new record April snowfall of 21.5 inches.||Impacts from this storm were numerous. All roads across the southwest, south central, and southeast were under no travel advisories due to the heavy snow. Interstate 94 was closed from the Montana border to Jamestown, and Highway 83 was closed from Bismarck to Underwood.  In addition, multiple school closings occurred on the 15th due to the heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow combined with winds gusting to 40 miles per hour to create a blizzard for much of the day on the 14th.

The peak of the storm occurred on Sunday when much of west and central North Dakota received heavy amounts of snow, with portions of the south central and southeast having a blizzard.||A combination of favorable moisture feed and strong surface and mid level dynamics brought historic snowfall to parts of west and central North Dakota. A moist conveyer belt extended from the eastern portions of Iowa and Illinois back northwest into North Dakota, situated along and north of an inverted surface trough stretched from southeast to northwest across South Dakota. Combined with strong diffluent flow aloft associated with the upper level low, snowfall rates approached two inches per hour across south central North Dakota on Sunday.||In addition to the heavy snow, strong surface winds developed across south central North Dakota west of the Missouri River and into the James River Valley. Wind gusts to 40 miles per hour combined with the heavy snow resulting in a blizzard.||Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 15 to 22 inches from the James River Valley into the Missouri River Valley. Lesser amounts were reported across the far west and north, with up to eight inches at Watford City, and around an inch near the Canadian border. The highest snowfall amounts were reported at Center and Flasher with 22 inches.  A record snowfall for any calendar day of the year was set at Bismarck, with 15.5 inches falling on April 14th. The storm total in Bismarck was 17.7 inches. This also resulted in setting a new record April snowfall of 21.5 inches.||Impacts from this storm were numerous. All roads across the southwest, south central, and southeast were under no travel advisories due to the heavy snow. Interstate 94 was closed from the Montana border to Jamestown, and Highway 83 was closed from Bismarck to Underwood.  In addition, multiple school closings occurred on the 15th due to the heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow combined with winds gusting to 40 miles per hour to create a blizzard for much of the day on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface low pressure system over southern Canada exited to the east, strong west to northwest winds pushed in behind it. The strongest gusts occurred over the higher elevations of northeast North Dakota along the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 40 mph occurred, with a peak wind of 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface low pressure system over southern Canada exited to the east, strong west to northwest winds pushed in behind it. The strongest gusts occurred over the higher elevations of northeast North Dakota along the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds of 59 mph occurred across much of northern Towner County from just east of Rolla through Rocklake. Peak winds of 51 knots were measured at the Rolla airport around 1438 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region remained under the influence of a stacked low pressure system over southeast North Dakota. A narrow band of three to five inches of rain fell across the Park River area, which had been hit with several rounds of steady rainfall in the past month. Numerous roads were closed due to high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding was noted in the city of Park River with ditches and field flooding outside of town. The city storm sewer lift stations were unable to keep up with water due to closed sewer flap gates and ongoing river flooding. A report of 4.85 inches of rain was received from Park River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted west and central North Dakota March 3rd into the evening of March 4th. Heavy snow fell northwest, north central, and into the northern James River Valley, while blizzard conditions developed March 4th across much of the rest of the west and south central parts of the state.||Initially the heavy snow was confined to the northwest, north central, and into Foster County, where six to twelve inches fell. The heaviest snow, one foot, was reported around Minot.||As the storm moved east, very strong northwest winds up to 55 miles per hour developed across the northwest during the afternoon of the 4th. The strong winds then continued to develop south across the remainder of western North Dakota during the afternoon and eventually into south central North Dakota during the evening hours. Wrap around snow accompanied the strong winds, with seven inches of snow reported across the far southwest, tapering off as you moved east along the South Dakota border.||The heavy snow and blizzard conditions resulted in the North Dakota Department of Transportation issuing no travel advisories for all of northern and western North Dakota at the peak of the storm.  Parts of Highway 85 and Highway 50 were closed in Williams and Divide Counties due to heavy snow drifts blocking the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresh snowfall, combined with northwest winds gusting over 45 miles per hour, resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and early evening of the 4th. No travel was advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of surface low pressure drifted to near Bismarck, North Dakota, by the early morning hours of the 4th and stalled there until the early morning hours of the 5th. This resulted in a prolonged period of snow over most of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Several stations in northeast North Dakota reported snow amounts of over a foot, with the Sarles (ND) station reporting 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 5 to 8 inches affected most of east central North Dakota. Areas of light freezing rain preceded the snow on Sunday evening leading to very icy roads and walkways.  Blowing and drifting snow persisted into Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of surface low pressure drifted to near Bismarck, North Dakota, by the early morning hours of the 4th and stalled there until the early morning hours of the 5th. This resulted in a prolonged period of snow over most of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Several stations in northeast North Dakota reported snow amounts of over a foot, with the Sarles (ND) station reporting 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of light freezing rain changed to heavy wet snow from Sunday evening into early Monday morning. This created very slippery conditions across portions of southeast North Dakota and led to prolonged road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moving out of the Rockies into the Northern Plains brought widespread snowfall amounts of one to four inches across North Dakota March 17th and 18th. An isolated six inch amount was reported at Stanley, Mountrail County.||On the back side of the storm strong northwest winds developed over western North Dakota the afternoon of the 17th, and spread into the central and east the evening of the 17th and into the morning of the 18th. Up to three inches of new snow fell and along with loose snow on the ground across the James River Valley, coupled with winds gusting at times over 45 miles per hour, resulting in a prolonged period of blizzard conditions the morning and afternoon of the 18th.||The blizzard prompted the North Dakota Department of Transportation to issue no travel advisories over eastern North Dakota during the worst of the storm on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total snowfall amount of six inches was reported at Stanley.

The peak of the storm occurred on Sunday when much of west and central North Dakota received heavy amounts of snow, with portions of the south central and southeast having a blizzard.||A combination of favorable moisture feed and strong surface and mid level dynamics brought historic snowfall to parts of west and central North Dakota. A moist conveyer belt extended from the eastern portions of Iowa and Illinois back northwest into North Dakota, situated along and north of an inverted surface trough stretched from southeast to northwest across South Dakota. Combined with strong diffluent flow aloft associated with the upper level low, snowfall rates approached two inches per hour across south central North Dakota on Sunday.||In addition to the heavy snow, strong surface winds developed across south central North Dakota west of the Missouri River and into the James River Valley. Wind gusts to 40 miles per hour combined with the heavy snow resulting in a blizzard.||Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 15 to 22 inches from the James River Valley into the Missouri River Valley. Lesser amounts were reported across the far west and north, with up to eight inches at Watford City, and around an inch near the Canadian border. The highest snowfall amounts were reported at Center and Flasher with 22 inches.  A record snowfall for any calendar day of the year was set at Bismarck, with 15.5 inches falling on April 14th. The storm total in Bismarck was 17.7 inches. This also resulted in setting a new record April snowfall of 21.5 inches.||Impacts from this storm were numerous. All roads across the southwest, south central, and southeast were under no travel advisories due to the heavy snow. Interstate 94 was closed from the Montana border to Jamestown, and Highway 83 was closed from Bismarck to Underwood.  In addition, multiple school closings occurred on the 15th due to the heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slope County received up to ten inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dropped heavy rain over portions of east central Cass county. Most rainfall reports ranged from two to four inches. This caused several roads to be shut down due to high water levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads in Fargo were water covered and closed. Sections of 12th Avenue North from the 3900 to 4200 block were closed with two feet of water covering it. Street and basement flooding was also reported in West Fargo. Two to five inches of rain was reported in a short amount of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface low pressure system over southern Canada exited to the east, strong west to northwest winds pushed in behind it. The strongest gusts occurred over the higher elevations of northeast North Dakota along the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 48 mph occurred, with a peak wind of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted west and central North Dakota March 3rd into the evening of March 4th. Heavy snow fell northwest, north central, and into the northern James River Valley, while blizzard conditions developed March 4th across much of the rest of the west and south central parts of the state.||Initially the heavy snow was confined to the northwest, north central, and into Foster County, where six to twelve inches fell. The heaviest snow, one foot, was reported around Minot.||As the storm moved east, very strong northwest winds up to 55 miles per hour developed across the northwest during the afternoon of the 4th. The strong winds then continued to develop south across the remainder of western North Dakota during the afternoon and eventually into south central North Dakota during the evening hours. Wrap around snow accompanied the strong winds, with seven inches of snow reported across the far southwest, tapering off as you moved east along the South Dakota border.||The heavy snow and blizzard conditions resulted in the North Dakota Department of Transportation issuing no travel advisories for all of northern and western North Dakota at the peak of the storm.  Parts of Highway 85 and Highway 50 were closed in Williams and Divide Counties due to heavy snow drifts blocking the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresh snowfall, combined with northwest winds gusting over 40 miles per hour, resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and early evening of the 4th. No travel was advised.

The peak of the storm occurred on Sunday when much of west and central North Dakota received heavy amounts of snow, with portions of the south central and southeast having a blizzard.||A combination of favorable moisture feed and strong surface and mid level dynamics brought historic snowfall to parts of west and central North Dakota. A moist conveyer belt extended from the eastern portions of Iowa and Illinois back northwest into North Dakota, situated along and north of an inverted surface trough stretched from southeast to northwest across South Dakota. Combined with strong diffluent flow aloft associated with the upper level low, snowfall rates approached two inches per hour across south central North Dakota on Sunday.||In addition to the heavy snow, strong surface winds developed across south central North Dakota west of the Missouri River and into the James River Valley. Wind gusts to 40 miles per hour combined with the heavy snow resulting in a blizzard.||Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 15 to 22 inches from the James River Valley into the Missouri River Valley. Lesser amounts were reported across the far west and north, with up to eight inches at Watford City, and around an inch near the Canadian border. The highest snowfall amounts were reported at Center and Flasher with 22 inches.  A record snowfall for any calendar day of the year was set at Bismarck, with 15.5 inches falling on April 14th. The storm total in Bismarck was 17.7 inches. This also resulted in setting a new record April snowfall of 21.5 inches.||Impacts from this storm were numerous. All roads across the southwest, south central, and southeast were under no travel advisories due to the heavy snow. Interstate 94 was closed from the Montana border to Jamestown, and Highway 83 was closed from Bismarck to Underwood.  In addition, multiple school closings occurred on the 15th due to the heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grant County received eighteen inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted west and central North Dakota March 3rd into the evening of March 4th. Heavy snow fell northwest, north central, and into the northern James River Valley, while blizzard conditions developed March 4th across much of the rest of the west and south central parts of the state.||Initially the heavy snow was confined to the northwest, north central, and into Foster County, where six to twelve inches fell. The heaviest snow, one foot, was reported around Minot.||As the storm moved east, very strong northwest winds up to 55 miles per hour developed across the northwest during the afternoon of the 4th. The strong winds then continued to develop south across the remainder of western North Dakota during the afternoon and eventually into south central North Dakota during the evening hours. Wrap around snow accompanied the strong winds, with seven inches of snow reported across the far southwest, tapering off as you moved east along the South Dakota border.||The heavy snow and blizzard conditions resulted in the North Dakota Department of Transportation issuing no travel advisories for all of northern and western North Dakota at the peak of the storm.  Parts of Highway 85 and Highway 50 were closed in Williams and Divide Counties due to heavy snow drifts blocking the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresh snowfall, combined with northwest winds gusting over 40 miles per hour, resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and early evening of the 4th. No travel was advised.

A late season rapid snow melt caused flooding along the Souris (Mouse) River and Des Lacs River through north central North Dakota. Additionally, there were necessary releases from upstream dams in North Dakota and Saskatchewan, Canada, due to rapid snow melt there and additional heavy rains in May and early June. This caused the flooding to continue. Homes in the Project Road area near Burlington took on water. Many county and township roads near the river were damaged. This flood began in April and continued through May and into June as spring rains added water to the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This flooding, along the Souris (Mouse) River, was in part due to earlier snow melt runoff and releases from upstream dams reaching this part of Bottineau County. Heavy rains in June added to the high water. Damage was reported to roads and bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Devils Lake region during the evening of the 21st and progressed into the Red River Valley around midnight. In combination with strong upper level dynamics, a good low level jet, and modest warm advection, this event produced several reports of large hail and a few tornadoes. The storms generally weakened as they moved into northwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado snapped about six wooden power poles along county road 17 before it dissipated. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rain in three hours produced widespread street flooding, flooding of underpasses, storm sewer backup, and basement flooding across the Fargo area. Water was reported to be up to the bases of fire hydrants near 45th Street and 12th Avenue North, making the roads impassable. River levels in town were already above minor flood stage due to previous heavy rainfall in the area, which meant that storm sewer flap gates were closed and pumping stations were likely overwhelmed by the high local runoff rates.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain accompanied the strong winds.

The storms generally weakened as they moved into northwest Minnesota.

Thunderstorms formed over the Devils Lake region along a cold frontal boundary and low pressure system. Storms also initiated along the warm front which extended down into southeast North Dakota. Afternoon temperatures topped out in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70. Many reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rain were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm yards and fields were flooded near Christine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level disturbances in the southwest flow aloft helped to spark an episode of severe weather across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Numerous reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rain were received across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains produced widespread street flooding around Valley City. Power outages and rapid runoff also combined to produce flooding of storm drains and sewer backup issues. At least four homes had partially collapsed basement walls. About 6.30 inches of rain fell over a two to three hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season rapid snow melt caused flooding along the Souris (Mouse) River and Des Lacs River through north central North Dakota. Additionally, there were necessary releases from upstream dams in North Dakota and Saskatchewan, Canada, due to rapid snow melt there and additional heavy rains in May and early June. This caused the flooding to continue. Homes in the Project Road area near Burlington took on water. Many county and township roads near the river were damaged. This flood began in April and continued through May and into June as spring rains added water to the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This river flooding was in part a continuation from the spring flooding that began in April and continued through May and into June. Some river points remained above flood stage through this period. The heavy rains through May and into June kept high water flowing through the system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Devils Lake region during the evening of the 21st and progressed into the Red River Valley around midnight. In combination with strong upper level dynamics, a good low level jet, and modest warm advection, this event produced several reports of large hail and a few tornadoes. The storms generally weakened as they moved into northwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch diameter branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across far southern Canada brought moderate snow and strong northwest winds to North Dakota during the morning and early afternoon of February 18th. The worst conditions occurred over parts of north central North Dakota into the northern James River Basin, where one to four inches of snow combined with winds gusting to around 40 miles per hour. This resulted in blizzard conditions prompting the North Dakota Department of Transportation and the North Dakota Highway Patrol to issue no travel advisories from Rolla through Jamestown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresh snow of around two inches and existing loose snow on the ground combined with northwest winds gusting over 35 miles per hour to create a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting northeast across the central U.S. brought heavy snow to the James River Basin in southeast North Dakota on the 10th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from around four inches over the western James River Basin of Logan and McIntosh counties to as much as ten inches at Oakes in Dickey County.||In addition to the snow, winds gusting to near forty miles per hour for up to twelve hours resulted in prolonged blizzard conditions. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches of new snowfall combined with frequent wind gusts to near forty miles per hour to result in prolonged blizzard conditions across McIntosh County. County officials advised no travel due to near zero visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across far southern Canada brought moderate snow and strong northwest winds to North Dakota during the morning and early afternoon of February 18th. The worst conditions occurred over parts of north central North Dakota into the northern James River Basin, where one to four inches of snow combined with winds gusting to around 40 miles per hour. This resulted in blizzard conditions prompting the North Dakota Department of Transportation and the North Dakota Highway Patrol to issue no travel advisories from Rolla through Jamestown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresh snow of around two inches and existing loose snow on the ground combined with northwest winds gusting over 35 miles per hour to create a blizzard.
